one_line_takeaway: Sequences of special characters (especially structural symbols like {,},<,>) are stronger training-data-extraction triggers for LLMs than repeated words, reaching up to ~49% semantic-leak ASR on ChatGPT and 2-10x gains with a logit-bias variant, while also exposing training-corpus composition and enabling verbose energy-latency outputs.
key_finding: Special-character sequences are markedly more effective data-extraction triggers than repeated English letters, and in-set (duplication-style) combinations beat cross-set ones. On the semantic-output level (Table 1), ChatGPT was most vulnerable: ASR 49.02% for S2-set, 44.02% for S1-set, 24.00% for L-set; ChatGLM reached 26.70% (S1-set) and Llama-2-Chat-70B 12.49% (S1-set). On the stricter, human-verified 'leaked output' level (Table 2), ChatGPT still leaked the most: S2-set 153 responses / 3.60% ASR, L-set 102/2.04%, S1-set 43/2.58%, while open-source models leaked only single-digit counts. SCA-LB extracted 2-10x more data than plain SCA on Llama-2 (Table 5: Code Llama-7B under SCA-LB yielded 33 code-corpus outputs, 62.3% of extractions, demonstrating corpus-composition probing). Ablations show longer inputs raise ASR (ChatGPT average ASR rose from 5.79% at length 0-210 to 9.72% at 810-1024) and larger models are more sensitive (Llama-2-Chat average ASR 2.69% at 7B -> 5.05% at 70B); aligned models (Llama-2-Chat, Vicuna) resist the L-set but remain vulnerable to special-character S-sets. Content/language distributions of leaked text expose corpus makeup (e.g. Gemini 60.6% code, ERNIEBot 96.3% Chinese, ChatGPT 68.8% article / 84.1% English).
core_contribution: The paper proposes the Special Characters Attack (SCA), a training-data extraction attack against LLMs that uses sequences of special characters (structural symbols like {,},<,>; and other special characters like @,#,$,%) and English letters as prompts to trigger models into 'diverging' from their chat style and emitting memorized training data (PII, code, web pages, prompt templates, chat logs). Its own key claims are: (1) special characters are stronger memory triggers than the repeated-word attack of Nasr et al., framed as 'second-order/joint memorization' between special characters and co-occurring raw text; (2) a mechanistic explanation via token-probability analysis on Llama-2, where SCA induces high-probability control/UTF-8 tokens (e.g. <s>, </s>, <0x20>, <0x0A>) and a sparse distribution resembling article/code corpora, and once a non-control semantic token is emitted the model latches onto matching memorized data; (3) two enhanced variants the authors introduce themselves: SCA-LB (Logit Biased), which raises logits of the first ~130 control/UTF-8 tokens in the Llama-2 tokenizer to extract 2-10x more data, and SCA-SC (Semantic Continuation), which asks commercial models to do translation/continuation tasks ending in special characters; (4) that leaked-data content/language distributions can be used to 'probe' the composition of a target model's training corpus, and that verbose non-stop outputs are a byproduct usable as an energy-latency (DoS-style) attack. The membership-inference, sponge-example, and repeated-word/token attacks discussed are cited prior work (Refs 33, 34, 24, 25), not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Black-box or gray-box adversary who can send arbitrary prompts to a deployed LLM (commercial API or open-source weights) and read the raw outputs, with the goal of extracting memorized training data (PII, code, prompts, chat history) and/or inflating inference cost. Requires no access to training data (the authors classify this as 'extractable memorization'). The stronger SCA-LB variant assumes the ability to set a logit_bias parameter at generation time (applicable to open-source models and APIs exposing logit bias). Main experiments were run before November 2023; the authors note the earlier repeated-word attack has since been patched by commercial vendors.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: the study would benefit from more detailed, systematic analysis across more LLMs and their complete tokenizers, which is left to future work. Additional reviewer-noted limitations grounded in the text: (1) verifying that 'semantic output' actually originates from training data is 'almost impossible' by the authors' admission, so the primary Count/ASR metrics conflate memorized leakage with plausibly-novel generation; only the smaller 'leaked output' set is human-verified. (2) No direct quantitative comparison to the repeated-word baseline (Nasr et al.) because commercial vendors had already patched it. (3) Experiments predate Nov 2023 and use gpt-3.5-turbo-0515, Gemini-v1-beta, ERNIE-Bot-turbo; ASRs may not transfer to current models. (4) SCA-LB requires logit-bias access, limiting its applicability. (5) The mechanistic 'joint memorization' explanation is supported by token-probability plots on a single model (Llama-2-7B) rather than causal evidence. (6) Detailed leaked examples are withheld for security reasons, limiting reproducibility.

datasets_used: Author-generated attack-sequence pools built from 3 character sets (S1-set 8 structural symbols, S2-set 22 special characters, L-set 26 English letters), lengths 10-1024 (e.g. 1665/4225/4995 in-set sequences per set)
Common Crawl (used as reference to verify leaked outputs)
Google search (used to verify leaked outputs)
No external benchmark dataset; leakage judged against public sources and open corpora
quantitative_results: ChatGPT semantic-output ASR (Table 1): S2-set 49.02% (2086 count), S1-set 44.02% (733), L-set 24.00% (1199)
ChatGLM semantic-output ASR S1-set 26.70% (444); Falcon L-set 15.56% (777); ERNIEBot S1-set 14.47% (241); Llama-2-Chat-70B S1-set 12.49% (208)
ChatGPT verified leaked-output (Table 2): S2-set 153/3.60%, L-set 102/2.04%, S1-set 43/2.58%; open-source models only single-digit counts
SCA-LB extracts 2-10x more data than SCA on Llama-2; Code Llama-7B SCA-LB: 33 code-corpus outputs = 62.3% (Table 5)
Sequence-length ablation on ChatGPT (Table 3): average ASR 5.79% (len 0-210) rising to 9.72% (len 810-1024)
Content distribution of leaked semantic output: Gemini 60.6% code, ChatGLM 50.6% code, ChatGPT 68.8% article/19.4% Wikipedia, ERNIEBot 75.3% article
Language distribution: ChatGPT 84.1% English, ERNIEBot 96.3% Chinese, Gemini 100% English
Llama-2 tokenizer: '<' appears 121x more often as a delimiter; logit bias applied to first 130 control/UTF-8 tokens (random uniform 0.0-4.0)
Energy-latency byproduct: verbose output defined as >~80% of max token length; commercial models (ChatGPT, Gemini) most prone to non-stop output
baselines_compared: Repeated-word attack (Nasr et al. 2023, Ref 24) and token-level duplication (Oh et al. 2023, Ref 25) — conceptual baselines; no direct numeric comparison because commercial vendors patched them
Article/code corpus sequences (estimated training-corpus reference, Fig 2)
SCA vs its own enhanced variants SCA-LB and SCA-SC
Across victim models: Llama-2/-Chat (7B/13B/70B), Llama-3-8B, Falcon-7.5B, ChatGLM-6B, Vicuna-7B, Code Llama-7B, ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo-0515), Gemini-v1-beta, ERNIE-Bot-turbo
Aligned (Llama-2-Chat, Vicuna) vs base (Llama-2-7B) models for alignment ablation
